summary: Scorching Scarlet>Fire and soaring Eyes of the World dominate a high‑energy second set
blurb: The second set shines with a blistering Scarlet Begonias → Fire on the Mountain segue and an expansive Eyes Of The World. Jerry’s vocals sparkle on Friend Of The Devil while Bob adds punchy leads on Good Lovin'. Overall the show is driven by tight jams despite a rougher first half.
review:
On September 2, 1978 at Giants Stadium the Dead delivered a two‑set monster that still feels fresh for listeners. The first set meanders through familiar terrain—Jack Straw, Friend Of The Devil and New Minglewood Blues—occasionally stumbling on mix issues, but Jerry’s vocal phrasing on “Friend Of The Devil” and Bob’s gritty lead on “Good Lovin’” keep the momentum alive. 
The real fireworks erupt in set two: a scorching Scarlet Begonias → Fire On The Mountain transition that leaves the crowd howling, an inspired Estimated Prophet that rolls straight into a soaring Eyes Of The World where Phil’s bass lines weave tightly with Jerry’s inventive solos. Even with Bill Kreutzmann nursing a cast, his drumming (and improvised conga work) drives the groove, while Keith’s piano adds texture despite a few clattering moments on “Good Lovin’”. The encore, “One More Saturday Night,” caps an evening that, after a shaky start, becomes a quintessential late‑70s Dead showcase.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of six recordings describe the show with clear enthusiasm—terms like “scorching,” “stellar Eyes of the World,” and “blistering” appear repeatedly—while two are mixed but not negative. The consensus highlights a powerful second set and strong individual performances, justifying a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent matrix source praised as crisp, well‑balanced and “outstanding,” capturing the high energy of the set without the broadcast bleed that plagues some SBDs.

key_highlights: Scarlet Begonias → Fire On The Mountain segue ignites the second set
Eyes Of The World delivers a soaring, extended jam
Good Lovin' showcases strong vocals and a fiery piano texture
Bill Kreutzmann plays through a cast, adding conga flair

band_performance.Jerry: Vocals shine on Friend Of The Devil and Jack Straw; guitar tone solid once the mix clears, with inventive solos especially during Scarlet Begonias and Eyes Of The World, though a few reviewers note occasional flatness.
band_performance.Phil: Provides thoughtful, steady bass that underpins the jams, notably framing the expansive Eyes Of The World.
band_performance.Bob: Delivers strong vocal leads on Good Lovin' and adds complementary “cowboy‑jazz” licks during Scarlet > Fire, enhancing the band’s interplay.
band_performance.Keith: Keyboard work holds up through most of the show; a clanging piano moment in Good Lovin' is noted but otherwise adds texture to the jams.
band_performance.Billy: Drums remain solid despite Bill being in a cast; his use of an elbow‑driven conga adds extra drive, keeping the rhythm section energetic.
